dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 1 for a transfer at [COMPANY_NAME] ED.
Hi, I'm Sam, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What's the reason for the call today?
Chief complaint is altered mental status. She has right-sided weakness and facial drooping.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and where we are?
Eyes are open and reactive, per documentation.
Can you tell me the name of the city we're in?
She's unable to lift her right arm. Right-sided weakness is confirmed.
Respiratory rate and depth are normal, per documentation, at 16 breaths per minute.
Pulse is 80 beats per minute, regular. Bilateral radial pulses are strong.
Blood pressure is 197/111 mmHg, per initial documentation.
Oxygen saturation is 97%. She is on 2 liters of oxygen via nasal cannula, as previously administered by ED staff.
Initial blood glucose was 250 mg/dL, recorded at 12:48, as documented.
She was driving when she suddenly slumped forward. Her son had to take over and drive her to the hospital.
She was last known well 1.5 hours before the call to EMS.
She does not report pain; a pain level of zero is noted in documentation.
4-lead ECG was performed and shows sinus rhythm.
Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color, as per documentation.
GCS score is 14. She is alert but disoriented.
Breath sounds are clear. No distress noted.
She has right-side facial droop. She is unable to grip or lift her right hand.
She is aphasic and unable to form clear words or concepts.
She vomited before she arrived at the ED.
She has a past medical history of stroke, CHF, and hypertension, as recorded in the ePCR.
She was given rectal aspirin and 4 mg Zofran by ED personnel prior to our arrival.
Field impression is stroke, likely CVA infarction. Protocol for altered mental status is initiated.
Pulse is now 79 bpm and regular, reported at 12:58.
Blood pressure is now 209/141 mmHg, per documentation at 12:58.
Oxygen saturation remains unchanged at 97%.
A blood glucose recheck was not performed, as it is not documented in the EPCR.
EKG continues to show sinus rhythm. No rhythm changes noted during transport.
Sinus rhythm persists with no new findings identified during transport.
Latest blood pressure is 212/102 mmHg, documented at 13:04:58.
Heart rate has dropped to 76 bpm during transport.
No medications administered by our team. RCH provided rectal aspirin and Zofran prior to our arrival.
The patient had two 18G IV lines placed at RCH, at the left AC and right wrist. Lines remain secure and patent.
We are transporting her to [COMPANY_NAME] for interventional neurology care.
Patient is secured on the stretcher with a 5-point harness for safety.
Right leg can still move, right arm and hand remain weak. RCH reported a CTA finding of large vessel occlusion.
Both 18G IVs are patent. She continues on 2 L/min oxygen via nasal cannula during transit. Final spo2 is 99%.
Transport was uneventful. All prior meds and procedures were accounted for and followed protocol.
The patient was directly taken to the angio-cath suite for urgent neurologic intervention.
The case is complete. Care transitions are now under hospital protocols and interventional teams.
